turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor has the opinion of the Court today in Case 24-154, Catholic Charities Bureau v. New Wisconsin Labor and Industry Review Commission.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Wisconsin, like many other states, exempts certain religious entities from paying unemployment compensation taxes. One such exemption covers nonprofits that are controlled by a church and "operated primarily for religious purposes". Petitioners, Catholic Charities Bureau and four of the entities that it operates, claim that they qualify for the exemption as religious organizations controlled by the Roman Catholic Church and whose charitable work is motivated by church doctrine. The Wisconsin Supreme Court disagreed. It concluded that petitioners are not operated primarily for religious purposes within the meaning of the statute because they neither inculcate religious faith nor serve only Catholics in their charitable work. The Court today holds that Wisconsin's tax exemption, as applied to petitioner, violates the First Amendment. The Wisconsin Supreme Court's interpretation of the state's exemption turned on inherently religious choices about how to perform charitable work, much like decisions about whether to baptize members or hold services on Sundays. Wisconsin's regime thus explicitly differentiates between religions based on theological practices, thereby triggering strict scrutiny. Yet the state has not met its burden to justify the law's application under that demanding standard, which requires narrow tailoring to a compelling state interest. First, Wisconsin asserts an interest in ensuring unemployment coverage for its citizens. The lines drawn by the exemption, however, are underinclusive. The statute exempts over 40 forms of employment from its compensation program including employment at religious entities that provide services similar to petitioners, when the work is done directly by the church as opposed to a nonprofit affiliated corporation. Second, Wisconsin claims an interest in avoiding entanglement in employment decisions based on religious doctrine. However, the exemption is overinclusive as to that goal. It covers all employees working at qualified organizations, not just those who inculcate religious faith or engage in other types of religious work relevant to the state's entanglement concerns. For these and other reasons set forth in our opinion, the Court reverses and remands for further proceedings consistent with the opinion. The decision of the Court is unanimous. Justices Thomas and Jackson have each filed concurring opinions.
